text:
There is something worth examining in the beauty of grand symmetry, the kind that announces itself immediately and asks nothing of the viewer. Think of the perfectly proportioned face, the flawless sunset photograph, the cathedral facade that hits every expected note. This kind of beauty tends to receive the most praise precisely because it requires the least work to appreciate. It is legible to everyone at once, which makes it feel universal and therefore profound, but that universality is partly what hollows it out.

The trouble with beauty that is immediately and completely available is that it has nowhere to take you. You register it, you feel the appropriate lift, and then it is done. There is no argument being made, no texture to return to, no secondary layer that rewards a second look. It is consummated on first contact.

Compare that to the beauty people tend to underrate: the beauty of something slightly wrong, slightly worn, slightly strange. A voice that is not conventionally pretty but carries some quality that lodges in you. A building that was never quite finished. A face that is genuinely odd until one day it is the only face you can imagine. These things cost you something in attention, and the cost is part of what makes the payoff real.

So my answer would be the beauty of perfect visual harmony, particularly in landscapes and human faces, when that harmony is smooth and frictionless and designed to be immediately agreed upon. It earns a reputation for depth that it often has not actually earned. The experience of recognizing it is sometimes mistaken for the experience of being genuinely moved by it, and those are quite different things.
